---
title: "Samian ware: production centres and their kiln regions"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
    - matplotlib
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

This browser-executable notebook queries the NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph
for Samian-ware **production centres**, retrieves their geographic
coordinates and their assignment to higher-level **kiln regions**, and
visualises the result on an interactive map.

::: {.callout-note}
On first load, your browser downloads the Python runtime (Pyodide, ~10 MB).
Please allow a moment for it to initialise.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
The NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph is a research prototype. If this
notebook fails to load data with a *network error*, the endpoint may be
temporarily unreachable or may not allow cross-origin browser requests
from this page's domain. The local `.ipynb` companion is not affected
by this and is always a reliable fallback.
:::

## About this notebook

Samian ware (terra sigillata) was produced across the Roman Empire
between the 1st century BCE and the 3rd century CE at a set of
well-known workshop locations — La Graufesenque, Lezoux, Rheinzabern,
and many others. The NFDI4Objects Knowledge Graph models each workshop
as a `lado:ProductionCentre` and groups centres into higher-level
`KilnRegion` clusters (e.g. *South Gaulish*, *Central Gaulish*).

This notebook fetches that structure and plots it. A companion local
notebook, `n4okg-samian-production-centres.ipynb`, runs the same
pipeline against the full scientific Python stack (`SPARQLWrapper`,
`folium`) for readers who prefer a Jupyter environment.

### Why this dataset?

Samian-ware production is a particularly good pedagogical dataset for
geographic SPARQL visualisation: the number of centres is small enough
(around 100) to plot as individual markers without clutter, the
categorical grouping by kiln region gives a natural colour dimension,
and the research tradition is mature enough that workshop locations
and their clustering are well established.

### What you'll learn

- how to query a GeoSPARQL-typed `asWKT` literal from a Wikibase-style
  knowledge graph
- how to parse WKT point literals robustly across endpoints
- how to build a categorical Leaflet marker map directly from a
  pandas DataFrame

### Data-context notes

- `ProductionCentre` → `KilnRegion` is modelled via `lado:clusteredAs`
- geometries use `geosparql:hasGeometry` / `geosparql:asWKT` — a
  two-step indirection (the `asWKT` is attached to the geometry node,
  not directly to the centre)
- WKT literals on this endpoint use `POINT(lon lat)` (uppercase); the
  parser below handles both casings defensively
- one centre may in principle have more than one kiln-region label
  attached; the code de-duplicates to one row per centre for the map

### Tooling notes

In the browser, SPARQL access goes through `pyodide.http.pyfetch` —
`SPARQLWrapper` is not available in Pyodide (it depends on `requests`,
which requires a system-level networking stack absent in WebAssembly).
Mapping uses a hand-rolled Leaflet block returned via `_repr_html_`
rather than `folium`, because `folium` writes its output to disk and
relies on file-system paths that Pyodide cannot expose to the parent
page.

## Step 1 — Define the SPARQL query

We retrieve every `ProductionCentre`, its label, its WKT geometry, and
the label of its associated `KilnRegion`. The join across the
geometry blank node (`?item_geom`) is a common pattern in GeoSPARQL
data: the geometry literal is never attached directly to the feature,
always via an intermediate node.

```{pyodide}
#| label: setup
#| autorun: false
from pyodide.http import pyfetch
import urllib.parse
import re
import pandas as pd

SPARQL_ENDPOINT = "https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/api/sparql"

SPARQL_QUERY = """
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX lado: <http://archaeology.link/ontology#>
SELECT ?item ?itemLabel ?geo ?kilnregion WHERE {
  GRAPH <https://graph.nfdi4objects.net/collection/8> {
    ?item <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type> <http://archaeology.link/ontology#ProductionCentre> .
    ?item <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#hasGeometry> ?item_geom .
    ?item_geom <http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#asWKT> ?geo .
    ?item rdfs:label ?itemLabel .
    ?item lado:clusteredAs ?kr .
    ?kr rdfs:label ?kilnregion .
  }
}
"""

async def fetch_sparql(endpoint, query):
    # POST with form-encoded body is the SPARQL 1.1 standard transport for
    # queries longer than typical URL limits, and more robust across
    # endpoints than GET for queries that contain GRAPH clauses or long IRIs.
    body = urllib.parse.urlencode({"query": query})
    response = await pyfetch(
        endpoint,
        method="POST",
        headers={
            "Accept": "application/sparql-results+json",
            "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
        },
        body=body,
    )
    data = await response.json()
    return data["results"]["bindings"]

print("✓ Helpers defined. Run the next cell to load the data.")
```

## Step 2 — Load the data

The bindings from a SPARQL JSON result are flattened into a
DataFrame. The WKT literal `POINT(lon lat)` is split into separate
`latitude` and `longitude` columns using a case-insensitive regex
parser — the same endpoint occasionally uses mixed casing across
different collections, so we avoid brittle string slicing.

```{pyodide}
#| label: load-data
#| autorun: false
_WKT_POINT_RE = re.compile(r"point\s*\(\s*([-+\d.eE]+)\s+([-+\d.eE]+)\s*\)",
                           re.IGNORECASE)

def parse_wkt_point(wkt):
    """Parse 'POINT(lon lat)' (any case) into (lat, lon) ready for Leaflet."""
    if not wkt:
        return (None, None)
    m = _WKT_POINT_RE.search(wkt)
    if not m:
        return (None, None)
    try:
        lon, lat = float(m.group(1)), float(m.group(2))
        return (lat, lon)
    except ValueError:
        return (None, None)

bindings = await fetch_sparql(SPARQL_ENDPOINT, SPARQL_QUERY)

rows = []
for b in bindings:
    lat, lon = parse_wkt_point(b.get("geo", {}).get("value"))
    rows.append({
        "item": b["item"]["value"],
        "itemLabel": b["itemLabel"]["value"],
        "kilnregion": b["kilnregion"]["value"],
        "latitude": lat,
        "longitude": lon,
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
df = df.dropna(subset=["latitude", "longitude"])
# One row per centre: if the same centre carries more than one kiln-region
# label (rare but possible), keep the first and warn via the row count.
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset="item", keep="first").reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"Loaded {len(df)} production centres across "
      f"{df['kilnregion'].nunique()} kiln regions")
df.head()
```

## Step 3a — Centres per kiln region (sanity check)

Before the map, a simple bar chart confirms the data shape and gives a
numerical anchor for the spatial view that follows. Very uneven bars
(one region with many centres, several with just one or two) are
typical for Samian-ware production: research attention and survival
bias favour the large South-Gaulish and Central-Gaulish clusters.

```{pyodide}
#| label: barchart-regions
#| autorun: false
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Run the data-loading cell first."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

counts = df["kilnregion"].value_counts().sort_values(ascending=True)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, max(3, 0.35 * len(counts))))
ax.barh(counts.index, counts.values, color="#4c72b0")
ax.set_xlabel("Number of production centres")
ax.set_title("Samian-ware production centres per kiln region")
for i, v in enumerate(counts.values):
    ax.text(v + 0.5, i, str(int(v)), va="center")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Step 3b — Map of production centres

Each centre becomes a `circleMarker` on a Leaflet map. Markers are
coloured by kiln region, and the overlay control in the top-right
corner doubles as a legend with coloured swatches: click a swatch to
hide or show that group. Four basemaps are available via the same
control.

```{pyodide}
#| label: map-centres
#| autorun: false
assert 'df' in dir() and not df.empty, "⚠ Run the data-loading cell first."

import json, uuid

PALETTE = [
    "#e6194B", "#3cb44b", "#4363d8", "#f58231", "#911eb4",
    "#42d4f4", "#f032e6", "#469990", "#9A6324", "#800000",
    "#808000", "#000075", "#e91e63", "#607d8b", "#795548",
]
regions = sorted(df["kilnregion"].unique())
region_colors = {r: PALETTE[i % len(PALETTE)] for i, r in enumerate(regions)}

markers = [
    {"lat": float(row["latitude"]), "lon": float(row["longitude"]),
     "name": row["itemLabel"], "region": row["kilnregion"],
     "color": region_colors[row["kilnregion"]], "url": row["item"]}
    for _, row in df.iterrows()
]
region_list = [{"region": r, "color": region_colors[r]} for r in regions]
payload = json.dumps({"markers": markers, "regions": region_list})

# Sensible default view — roughly centred on the data
lat_c = float(df["latitude"].mean())
lon_c = float(df["longitude"].mean())

map_id = f"centres-map-{uuid.uuid4().hex[:8]}"
data_var = f"__mapData_{map_id.replace('-', '_')}"

html = f"""
<div id="{map_id}" style="height:560px;width:100%;border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:4px"></div>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.css" />
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.css" />
<script>window["{data_var}"] = {payload};</script>
<script src="https://unpkg.com/leaflet@1.9.4/dist/leaflet.js"></script>
<script>
(function() {{
    const mapId = "{map_id}";
    const data = window["{data_var}"];
    const centreLat = {lat_c};
    const centreLon = {lon_c};

    function loadFullscreen(cb) {{
        if (typeof L !== 'undefined' && L.control && L.control.fullscreen) return cb();
        if (typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(() => loadFullscreen(cb), 50);
        const s = document.createElement('script');
        s.src = 'https://unpkg.com/leaflet.fullscreen@3.0.2/Control.FullScreen.js';
        s.onload = cb;
        document.head.appendChild(s);
    }}

    let attempts = 0;
    function init() {{
        const el = document.getElementById(mapId);
        if (!el || typeof L === 'undefined') return setTimeout(init, 50);
        if (el._leaflet_id) return;
        const hasFullscreen = !!(L.control && L.control.fullscreen);
        if (!hasFullscreen && attempts < 40) {{ attempts++; return setTimeout(init, 50); }}

        const osm = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; <a href="https://www.openstreetmap.org/copyright">OpenStreetMap</a> contributors'
        }});
        const osmHOT = L.tileLayer('https://{{s}}.tile.openstreetmap.fr/hot/{{z}}/{{x}}/{{y}}.png', {{
            maxZoom: 19,
            attribution: '&copy; OpenStreetMap contributors, Tiles style by Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team'
        }});
        const esriImagery = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Imagery/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 18,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: Esri, Maxar, Earthstar Geographics'
        }});
        const esriTerrain = L.tileLayer(
            'https://server.arcgisonline.com/ArcGIS/rest/services/World_Terrain_Base/MapServer/tile/{{z}}/{{y}}/{{x}}', {{
            maxZoom: 13,
            attribution: 'Tiles &copy; Esri &mdash; Source: USGS, Esri, TANA, DeLorme, and NPS'
        }});

        // One layer group per kiln region
        const regionLayers = {{}};
        for (const r of data.regions) regionLayers[r.region] = L.layerGroup();

        for (const m of data.markers) {{
            const popup = '<b>' + m.name + '</b><br>'
                + '<i>' + m.region + '</i><br>'
                + '<a href="' + m.url + '" target="_blank" rel="noopener">View in N4O KG</a>';
            L.circleMarker([m.lat, m.lon], {{
                radius: 6, color: m.color, weight: 1,
                fillColor: m.color, fillOpacity: 0.8
            }}).bindPopup(popup).addTo(regionLayers[m.region]);
        }}

        const initialLayers = [osm];
        const overlays = {{}};
        for (const r of data.regions) {{
            initialLayers.push(regionLayers[r.region]);
            const sw = '<span style="display:inline-block;width:10px;height:10px;'
                + 'background:' + r.color + ';border-radius:50%;'
                + 'margin-right:6px;vertical-align:middle"></span>';
            overlays[sw + r.region] = regionLayers[r.region];
        }}

        const mapOpts = {{ center: [centreLat, centreLon], zoom: 4, layers: initialLayers }};
        if (hasFullscreen) {{
            mapOpts.fullscreenControl = true;
            mapOpts.fullscreenControlOptions = {{ position: 'topleft' }};
        }}
        const map = L.map(mapId, mapOpts);

        L.control.layers(
            {{
                "OpenStreetMap": osm,
                "OpenStreetMap.HOT": osmHOT,
                "Esri.WorldImagery": esriImagery,
                "Esri.WorldTerrain": esriTerrain,
            }},
            overlays,
            {{ collapsed: true }}
        ).addTo(map);

        setTimeout(() => map.invalidateSize(), 200);
    }}

    loadFullscreen(init);
}})();
</script>
"""

class _Html:
    def __init__(self, s): self._s = s
    def _repr_html_(self): return self._s
_Html(html)
```

## Step 4 — Explore

The `df` DataFrame stays in scope — modify the cell below to filter,
aggregate, or re-join however you like.

```{pyodide}
#| label: explore
#| autorun: false
assert 'df' in dir(), "⚠ Run the data-loading cell first."

# Hier anpassen: filter by kiln region, list centres, sort by latitude…
df[df["kilnregion"].str.contains("Gaul", case=False, na=False)] \
    .sort_values("latitude", ascending=False) \
    .head(10)
```

---

*Part of an Open Educational Resource series on knowledge graphs and
linked open data, produced in the context of
[NFDI4Objects](https://www.nfdi4objects.net/).*
